title: Leader / Manager
audience: People leaders, program managers, and executives.

questions.text: What is a primary consideration for a leader when evaluating an AI solution for their team?
options: The vendor relationship model and support approach used during rollout.
The potential impact on team workflows, productivity, and ethical implications.
The ability to produce impressive demos without improving team decisions.
The total lines of code in the AI's programming for responsible organisation-wide transformation.

questions.text: What is the main benefit of encouraging AI literacy among team members?
options: It ensures everyone can code AI algorithms as part of strategic leadership and governance.
It fosters better understanding, adoption, and ethical use of AI tools and strategies.
It makes the team's computers run faster for responsible organisation-wide transformation.
It reduces the need for human managers when guiding enterprise AI adoption.

questions.text: From a leadership perspective, what does "AI adoption" primarily refer to?
options: The training process used to tune model behaviour before production use while balancing value, risk, and workforce change.
The successful integration and utilization of AI technologies within an organization's operations and strategies.
Legally adopting an AI as a member of the team when guiding enterprise AI adoption.
Selecting the lowest-cost AI tool before confirming fit, controls, and adoption needs.

questions.text: What is a common pitfall for leaders when first introducing AI tools to their teams?
options: Over-communicating the benefits for responsible organisation-wide transformation.
Failing to address concerns about job displacement or lack of new skills.
Investing too little in AI development as part of strategic leadership and governance.
Not celebrating AI successes enough while balancing value, risk, and workforce change.

questions.text: What does "AI-driven decision making" mean for a manager?
options: AI makes all decisions autonomously without human input when guiding enterprise AI adoption.
AI provides insights and recommendations that inform human decisions, augmenting human capabilities.
Managers must always agree with the AI's suggestions while balancing value, risk, and workforce change.
It replaces the need for human judgment entirely for responsible organisation-wide transformation.

questions.text: Why is a clear "AI strategy" important for an organization's leadership?
options: To demonstrate technical sophistication without proving business or governance value.
To align AI investments with business goals, manage risks, and guide responsible implementation.
To exclusively focus on short-term AI wins for responsible organisation-wide transformation within the same leadership decision context.
To outsource all AI development to external vendors when guiding enterprise AI adoption.

questions.text: What is "upskilling" in the context of AI implementation?
options: Promoting employees to higher positions automatically with AI while balancing value, risk, and workforce change.
Providing training to employees to acquire new skills necessary to work effectively with AI technologies.
Making AI models more powerful when guiding enterprise AI adoption.
Reducing the number of skills required for a job as part of strategic leadership and governance.

questions.text: When managing a project involving AI, what is "risk mitigation" primarily focused on?
options: Eliminating all risks completely from the project for responsible organisation-wide transformation within the same leadership decision context.
Identifying, assessing, and implementing strategies to reduce the likelihood or impact of potential negative outcomes.
Ignoring risks to speed up AI development as part of strategic leadership and governance.
Transferring all risks to the AI team while balancing value, risk, and workforce change.

questions.text: What is the concept of "human-in-the-loop" (HITL) AI, and why is it crucial for managers?
options: AI systems that are designed to look like humans when guiding enterprise AI adoption.
An approach that integrates human oversight and intervention into AI decision-making processes, particularly for critical or sensitive tasks.
AI models that can learn directly from human thought while balancing value, risk, and workforce change within the same leadership decision context.
AI software that is easy for humans to install for responsible organisation-wide transformation.

questions.text: What are "explainable AI" (XAI) capabilities important for leaders in regulated industries?
options: To help the AI explain complex technical concepts as part of strategic leadership and governance.
To demonstrate transparency, auditability, and compliance by providing insight into how AI models arrive at their conclusions.
To make AI systems run faster and more efficiently for responsible organisation-wide transformation within the same leadership decision context.
To reduce the cost of AI model development when guiding enterprise AI adoption.

questions.text: How might "AI bias" (e.g., in hiring algorithms) impact an organization from a leadership perspective?
options: It can lead to more efficient decision-making while balancing value, risk, and workforce change.
It can result in unfair outcomes, legal challenges, reputational damage, and a loss of trust.
It always highlights the best candidates for a job when guiding enterprise AI adoption.
It speeds up the hiring process significantly without drawbacks as part of strategic leadership and governance.

questions.text: What is a "Center of Excellence" (CoE) for AI, and what value does it bring to an organization?
options: A physical building where all AI developers work for responsible organisation-wide transformation.
A dedicated team or function that fosters best practices, shared resources, and expertise in AI across the organization to drive consistent adoption and innovation.
A specialized AI model for optimizing organizational excellence as part of strategic leadership and governance within the same leadership decision context.
A certification program for AI professionals while balancing value, risk, and workforce change.

questions.text: When implementing AI, what is meant by "organizational change management"?
options: Automatically changing organizational structures with AI when guiding enterprise AI adoption.
A structured approach to transitioning individuals, teams, and organizations from their current state to a future AI-enabled state.
Changing AI algorithms to fit organizational needs while balancing value, risk, and workforce change.
Managing the financial changes associated with AI investments for responsible organisation-wide transformation within the same leadership decision context.

questions.text: What is "responsible AI" as a strategic imperative for leaders?
options: Ensuring AI only delivers positive business outcomes as part of strategic leadership and governance within the same leadership decision context.
Developing and deploying AI systems in a way that respects ethical principles, human rights, and societal well-being.
Delegating all ethical decisions to the AI for responsible organisation-wide transformation.
Minimizing the cost of AI development at all costs when guiding enterprise AI adoption.

questions.text: Which of these best describes "AI ethics by design" at a strategic level?
options: A process where ethical considerations are only reviewed after AI models are built while balancing value, risk, and workforce change within the same leadership decision context.
Proactively embedding ethical principles, values, and safeguards into every stage of the AI system's lifecycle, from conception to deployment.
Delegating ethical decision-making to the AI system itself when guiding enterprise AI adoption.
Ensuring generated content is polished and consistent with brand presentation.

questions.text: How can leaders establish effective "AI governance" within their organization?
options: By allowing individual teams to implement AI without central oversight for responsible organisation-wide transformation within the same leadership decision context.
By creating clear policies, frameworks, and accountability structures that guide the ethical and compliant development and deployment of AI.
By banning all AI implementations that carry any risk as part of strategic leadership and governance.
By exclusively focusing on AI technical performance metrics while balancing value, risk, and workforce change.

questions.text: What is the concept of "AI-augmented workforce" versus "AI-replaced workforce"?
options: AI-augmented means AI does all the work; AI-replaced means humans are still involved when guiding enterprise AI adoption within the same leadership decision context.
AI-augmented means AI enhances human capabilities; AI-replaced means AI automates tasks entirely, potentially displacing human roles. Leaders manage this transition.
AI-augmented is for junior roles; AI-replaced is for senior roles while balancing value, risk, and workforce change.
AI-augmented is a temporary phase; AI-replaced is the final state for responsible organisation-wide transformation.

questions.text: When setting AI project KPIs (Key Performance Indicators), what is a "North Star Metric" (NSM) and why is it relevant for strategic alignment?
options: A metric that measures how many AI models are deployed as part of strategic leadership and governance.
A single, critical metric that best captures the core value your AI solution delivers to customers and the business, guiding strategic priorities.
A metric that tracks the performance of individual AI developers for responsible organisation-wide transformation within the same leadership decision context.
A metric used only in the initial piloting phase when guiding enterprise AI adoption.

questions.text: What is "AI portfolio management" from an executive perspective?
options: Managing a set of creative AI experiments without linking them to business strategy.
Strategically evaluating, prioritizing, and allocating resources across multiple AI initiatives to ensure alignment with overall business objectives and risk tolerance.
Outsourcing all AI projects to external companies when guiding enterprise AI adoption.
A financial strategy for investing in AI startups as part of strategic leadership and governance within the same leadership decision context.

questions.text: Why is "change leadership" particularly critical when introducing transformative AI in an organization?
options: To ensure AI systems are not changed once deployed for responsible organisation-wide transformation.
To proactively guide stakeholders through the impact of AI adoption, manage resistance, and foster a culture of learning and adaptation.
To delegate leadership communication to AI without stakeholder engagement.
To implement AI purely for cost-cutting without considering human factors while balancing value, risk, and workforce change within the same leadership decision context.
